note_data.title: Sets and functions
keyPoints: A set is an organised, unique collection of elements where you are not allowed to repeat the same element in a set
Set notation uses ':' which should be read as 'Such that', ℰ for Universal set, ∈ for 'is an element of', and ∉ for 'is not an element of'
A' represents the Complement of set A, which is the set containing all the elements of the universal set but those of set A
A ∪ B represents Union of A and B, which is the set produced by writing all the elements of both sets
A ∩ B represents Intersection of A and B, which is the set produced by noting down the elements common in both the sets
A ⊆ B means A is a subset of B, while A ⊂ B means A is a proper subset of B
The empty/null set is represented by ∅, and n(A) represents the number of elements in set A
Venn Diagrams can be used to visualize set relationships, showing unions, intersections, and complements graphically

definitions.definition: An organised, unique collection of elements where you are not allowed to repeat the same element in a set
The set produced by writing all the elements of both sets
The set produced by noting down the elements common in both the sets
The set containing all the elements of the universal set but those of set A

howToAnswer.instruction: Write elements separated by commas inside curly brackets { }
For composite functions, substitute the inner function into the outer function step by step
For Venn diagrams, draw circles inside a rectangle, label all regions clearly

modelAnswer.question: Given f(x) = 2x + 3 and g(x) = x², find (a) f⁻¹(x) (b) (g ∘ f)(5)
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct method for finding inverse

commonMistakes: Repeating the same element in a set when sets must contain unique elements only
Confusing subset notation A ⊆ B with proper subset notation A ⊂ B
Misreading the colon ':' in set notation instead of reading it as 'Such that'
examinerTips: Remember that you are not allowed to repeat the same element in a set
Read ':' as 'Such that' when interpreting set notation
formulas: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
If f(x) = ax + b, then f⁻¹(x) = (x-b)/a
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Venn diagram showing two overlapping circles A and B inside a rectangle (universal set ξ), with regions labeled: A only, B only, A∩B (intersection), and elements outside both circles
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to find the set of values of k for which kx² + (k+3)x + 8 = 0 has two distinct real roots using discriminant analysis
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q7 (5 marks) — students had to find set of values of k for which (x-k)|x-7|^(1/3) = 4 has exactly two real roots, requiring graphical understanding
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — candidates needed to find the set of values of x satisfying |2^(x+1) - 2| < 0.5 using modulus properties
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q5 (3 marks) — students were asked to find set of values of x for which binomial expansion |x| < 2 is valid

workedExample.question: Find the set of values of x satisfying |2^(x+1) - 2| < 0.5 (4 marks)
steps: Step 3: Take logarithm base 2: log₂(1.5) < x+1 < log₂(2.5)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for removing modulus correctly, 1 mark for isolating exponential term, 1 mark for taking logarithms, 1 mark for correct final answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused set notation with interval notation' — always use correct bracket notation: { } for sets, [ ] or ( ) for intervals
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently forgot to consider both positive and negative cases when solving modulus inequalities' — always split |f(x)| < k into -k < f(x) < k
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often gave single values instead of ranges when finding domains' — remember that set of values typically means an interval or union of intervals, not isolated points
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must master the number line representation showing solution sets with open/closed circles and shaded regions. For inequalities like a < x < b, use open circles at endpoints with shaded region between. For unions like x < a or x > b, show two separate shaded regions with a gap. Include proper labeling of critical values and clear indication of included/excluded boundaries.
note_data.quickRecap: Sets are organised, unique collections of elements with no repetition allowed. Key operations include union (all elements from both sets), intersection (common elements only), and complement (elements in universal set but not in the given set). Set notation uses specific symbols like ∪, ∩, and ' to represent these operations, with Venn diagrams providing visual representation of set relationships.
